​ 

Customer Churn Prediction  

Let me build an interactive step-by-step walkthrough of the entire pipeline for you. Here's a full step-by-step walkthrough of the project.  

 

Step 1 — Synthetic data generation 

Since we had no real dataset, we generated 5,000 customers programmatically using NumPy. Each customer gets 14 realistic attributes like tenure, monthly charges, contract type, and number of support calls. The key part is the churn score formula — it's a logistic equation where things like a month-to-month contract, fiber optic internet, or electronic check payment increase churn risk, while longer tenure and tech support reduce it. This mimics real-world telecom churn patterns. The output churn rate was 31.6%, which is realistic. 

 

Step 2 — Preprocessing 

Raw data isn't model-ready. Three things happen here. Binary columns like tech_support (Yes/No) get encoded to 0/1. Multi-class columns like contract_type and internet_service get one-hot encoded (e.g. contract_type_Month-to-month, contract_type_Two year). Then two new features are engineered: avg_monthly_spend (total charges ÷ tenure) captures per-month value, and tenure_bucket groups customers into 4 lifecycle bands. We end up with 23 features total. 

1

 

Step 3 — Train-test split + SMOTE 

The data is split 80/20 with stratify=y so both splits have the same churn ratio. Then SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) is applied only on the training set. Our training data had 2,734 retained vs 1,266 churned — imbalanced. SMOTE generates synthetic churned customers by interpolating between real ones, balancing it to 2,734 vs 2,734. This prevents the model from just predicting "retained" for everyone and hitting 68% accuracy without learning anything useful. 

 

Step 4 — Model training 

Two models are trained on the SMOTE-balanced data. Random Forest builds 300 decision trees independently and averages their votes — it's robust and interpretable via feature importances. XGBoost builds trees sequentially, each one correcting the errors of the previous one — it tends to squeeze out a bit more performance. Both use StandardScaler to normalize features so that high-range values like total_charges don't dominate low-range ones like senior_citizen. 

 

Step 5 — Evaluation + inference 

Four metrics are computed on the held-out test set. ROC-AUC measures how well the model separates churners from non-churners across all thresholds (both models scored ~0.96, which is

2

excellent). Average Precision focuses on the positive (churn) class. F1 balances precision and recall. Cross-validation (5-fold) tests stability — both models scored ~0.97-0.98 CV AUC with low variance, meaning they generalize well, not just memorizing training data. 

The inference demo runs a single high-risk customer profile (3-month tenure, month-to-month contract, fiber, senior, electronic check) and both models correctly flag it at ~99% churn probability. 

 

The key design decisions worth highlighting for your portfolio: using SMOTE only on training data (not test), stratified splits, and CV for generalization — these show ML engineering awareness beyond just fitting a model. 

 

​ 

3
